q: Governance is a decision-making framework that accelerates outcomes by defining which of the following? (Select all that apply)
options: What decisions need to be made and when
correct: What decisions need to be made and when
explanation: Governance defines what decisions need to be made and when, who is involved, and how decisions are made. 'How the work gets done' belongs to the operating model, not governance.

q: Which of the following are key components of the ServiceNow governance framework? (Select all that apply)
explanation: The ServiceNow governance framework has exactly three boards: Executive Steering Board (strategy), Demand Board (portfolio), and Technical Governance Board (technical). The other options are not part of this framework.

q: Which of the following are best practices when developing applications in ServiceNow? (Select all that apply)
options: Customize out-of-the-box scripts to match business needs
Follow a consistent naming convention across all artifacts
Use global scope for all applications to ensure broad access
Use established and officially supported patterns when working with ServiceNow
correct: Follow a consistent naming convention across all artifacts
Use established and officially supported patterns when working with ServiceNow
explanation: Consistent naming conventions and officially supported patterns are best practices. Customizing OOTB scripts increases technical debt and upgrade risk. Global scope should be avoided in favor of scoped applications.

q: Which of the following are best practice approaches for achieving continuous improvement in governance? (Select all that apply)
options: Analyze metrics to continuously drive process improvement and remove bottlenecks
Define metrics that measure the effectiveness of your governance process
Triage decisions that require the attention of governance boards and push all but the most important to your front lines
Conduct a thorough review of product documentation and release notes to identify governance policies
correct: Analyze metrics to continuously drive process improvement and remove bottlenecks
Define metrics that measure the effectiveness of your governance process
Triage decisions that require the attention of governance boards and push all but the most important to your front lines
explanation: Continuous governance improvement focuses on defining metrics, removing bottlenecks, and triaging decisions. Reviewing product documentation is useful but is not one of the three core CI approaches.

q: What is the difference between governance and an operating model?
a: Governance: decision-making framework — defines how ServiceNow strategy, portfolio, and technical decisions are made, and who has authority
Operating model: basic framework for how products and services reach consumers — governance is foundational within it, but not the same thing

q: Why should organizations invest in governance?
a: Drives transformation vision: aligns investments with strategic goals, ensures appropriate resource allocation
Delivers the right work at the right time: clear roles reduce roadblocks, enable faster decision-making
Maintains implementation integrity: provides guidelines, standards, and leading practices — data handled appropriately, systems secure, services reliable

q: What are the 3 governance boards in the ServiceNow framework and what does each address?
a: Executive Steering Board: strategic decisions — vision, budget, final roadmap (senior IT + business leaders)
Demand Board: portfolio decisions — scoping work, roadmap aligned with ESB vision (platform + process owners)
Technical Governance Board: technical decisions — standards and guardrails for platform development (IT leaders + architects)

q: What are the 6 key players in a ServiceNow governance program?
a: Executive Sponsor: chairs ESC, sets vision, secures funding
Platform Owner: strategic roadmap, governance policies, may chair TGB
Platform Architect: best practices for data modelling/integrations/configs, may chair TGB
Demand Manager: chairs demand board, demand prioritization
Process Owner: defines process best practices, approves process changes
Developer/Admin: impact assessment, documentation, change control

q: What are the 4 technical governance domains the TGB oversees (DEAP)?
a: Data Management: ownership, quality, security, integrations
Environment Management: instance structure, environment policies
App Development Management: customization guidelines, naming conventions, coding standards
Platform Management: access policies, upgrade management, performance monitoring

q: Why should custom development only be used when configuration cannot meet business needs?
a: Customization increases technical debt and reduces upgradeability. The key decision question: does the business value of customization outweigh the risk in terms of technical debt and upgrade complexity? Configuration is always preferred.

q: What is the ServiceNow leading practice for upgrade frequency?
a: Upgrade at least once per year. Many customers aim to upgrade with each new release to stay current, maintain SN support, and access new OOTB functionality.

q: What is the difference between a lagging and a leading governance indicator?
a: Lagging indicator: success against top-level program KPIs (e.g. did implementation deliver on expectations?) — hard to directly attribute to governance
Leading indicator: measures effectiveness of governance processes and policies over time (e.g. # of board decisions per quarter, % policy updates, escalation reduction)

q: What is the CTA's role in relation to the Technical Governance Board?
a: Help outline the board's charter, scope, roles, and responsibilities
Provide expertise on platform architecture, scalability, and maintainability
Help define coding standards, development guidelines, and security policies
Align governance with latest ServiceNow platform capabilities
Identify strategies for addressing technical debt, legacy issues, and inefficiencies

q: What are the two ServiceNow tools for platform health and what does each do?
a: ITOM Health: AI-driven monitoring, event correlation, and log analytics — proactively detects, diagnoses, and resolves IT infrastructure issues; Health Log Analytics predicts issues before they impact users
Instance Scan: interrogates instance for configurations indicating health issues and best practice violations — used in dev operations, release management, and pre/post upgrades

q: Why is the right amount of governance important — what happens with too little or too much?
a: Too little: similar indecision as having no governance, opens implementation to risks
Too much (bureaucratic): impedes responsiveness, blocks entrepreneurial thinking, creates 'red tape' that slows work unnecessarily
Goal: continuously review process and track metrics to find the right balance

q: What are the key governance golden rules recommended by ServiceNow?
a: Out-of-the-box first: adjust business before adjusting platform
Single system of record: only maintain CIs/attributes with actual consumption requirements
Outcomes over features: focus on business outcomes, not legacy features
80/20 rule: deliver 80% of immediate need, do not configure for edge cases
Upgrades: stay current within X months of each release
Integrations: only those needed for value; bespoke builds last resort

q: What upgrade management policy areas should be defined for a ServiceNow platform?
a: Process: upgrade flow, who is involved, when each environment is upgraded, skipped items remediation
Approvals: what approvals before/during/after upgrade
Testing: what testing before and after the upgrade
Scheduling: maintenance windows and blackout windows
Communications: before/during/after upgrade
Support: issue handling during upgrades, when to engage SN Support
